title: Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of Core-Shell Latex Particles by1H Solid-State NMR
abstract:
1262
Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of
Core-Shell Latex Particles by 1H Solid-State NMR
M. Valtier,1 X. Drujon,2 M. Wilhelm,1 H. W. Spiess* 1
1 Max-Planck-Institut fu¨r Polymerforschung, Postfach 3148, 55021 Mainz, Germany
2 ATOFINA, Groupement de Recherche de Lacq (GRL), B.P. 34, 64170 Lacq, France
Introduction
Composite latex particles are extensively used in many
fields such as paints, adhesives, coatings or impact modi-
fiers for other polymers. The combination of two poly-
mers with contrasted characteristics (soft/hard, hydropho-
bic/hydrophilic, etc.) at the sub-micronic scale makes the
design of materials with unique combinations of antago-
nistic properties (impact resistance/high modulus,[1] low
film formation temperature/low tack,[2] etc.) possible. The
precise control of the latex particle morphology is critical
to satisfy the end use requirements.
Seeded emulsion polymerisation is the most common
process for composite latexes synthesis, at the laboratory
scale as well as at the industrial scale. Unfortunately, this
preparation method can produce a wide range of different
latex
morphologies,[3–7] e.g.,
hemispherical
particles,
“raspberry”, “sandwich”, “mushroom” or “confetti-like”
structures. Among the many process parameters affecting
particle morphology[8] can be cited the nature and the
weight ratio of the two polymer phases, the presence of
difunctional monomers inducing the crosslinking of one
of the polymers, the mode of addition of the shell mono-
mer and of various additives such as surfactants and
initiators, or the formation of compatibilising grafted
copolymers.[9] In any case, the ideal core-shell structure,
where the second stage polymer covers the first stage
polymer with a complete phase separation, is never
obtained.[10] At best, an interphase containing a mixture
of the two polymers on a molecular level is created
between the core and the shell.
Several analytical methods have been developed to
characterise the morphology of composite latex particles.
Full Paper: Solid-state 1H NMR spin diffusion experi-
ments are used to characterise the inner morphology and
the core coverage of core-shell latex particles. From the
spin-diffusion curve a specific surface is calculated as a
function of the amount of mobile phase selected by an
adjustable dipolar filter. In this paper, a parameter g is
introduced to compare the specific surfaces of actual core-
shell particles with an idealistic model without any inter-
phase between mobile and rigid phases. With a simple
mathematical model it is possible to fit the variation of g
versus the radius of the particle. The quantitative results
obtained lead to the localisation of the interphase, the
measure of its thickness and give an idea of the profile of
molecular mobility along the radius of the particle. Five
samples are analysed with this new methodology to test
the accuracy and the sensitivity of the method. The influ-
ence of the core-shell ratio and the synthesis conditions on
the interphase structure and the core coverage factor are
discussed. Comparison between freeze-dried and spray-
dried samples and air-dried films shows measurable evo-
lution of the interphase morphology of the particles upon
drying. The measurements also suggest very close pack-
ing of the core-shell particles during the film formation
process.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2001, 202, No. 7
i WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69451 Weinheim 2001
1022-1352/2001/0704–1262$17.50+.50/0
Curves g(rA) measured on the freeze-dried sample E30 at
273, 298 and 323 K. As the measurement temperature
increases, the g(rA) curve is shrunk and shifted towards the
peripheral layers of the core shell particle. The highest inten-
sity of the curve decreases in the same way, which indicates
that the surface of the selection is getting smoother.
Macromol. Chem. Phys. 2001, 202, 1262–1272
 Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of Core-Shell Latex Particles ...
1263
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM)[11] is the most
widely used technique. Additional information can be
obtained by atomic force microscopy (AFM),[12] X-ray[13]
or neutron scattering,[14] as well as by soap titration.[15]
These methods often need specially treated, enriched
samples which tend to modify the particle structure itself
or affect the statistical averages to be measured. Despite
the industrial importance of composite latex, and the
abundant literature on the subject, it is still a very difficult
task to obtain quantitative information on the heterogene-
ities at the latex particle scale, especially with respect to
the inner structure of the particles. In contrast to the
above-mentioned methods, NMR spectroscopy is a non-
invasive technique, which has been used quite success-
fully in recent years to study composite particle morphol-
ogies. The interphase region thickness and average com-
position of core-shell latex was determined.[16–19] The
core coverage of similar particles was also determined.[20]
The aim of this paper is to present a new solid-state
NMR methodology that enables us to localise and quan-
tify the interphase in a core-shell system and to measure
its thickness. Hence it gives an idea of the molecular
mobility profile along the radius of the spherical particles.
To demonstrate the value of this new methodology, we
applied it to particularly problematic latex samples, char-
acterised by a small particle size, a prevailing elastomeric
core and a thin thermoplastic shell.
Another objective was to study the influence of the
sample preparation method (freeze-drying coagulation,
spray drying, or filmification at room temperature of the
original emulsion) on the evolution of the particle mor-
phology.
Experimental Part
Samples
Five latex of core-shell morphology have been provided by
ATOFINA (Research Group of Lacq, France). They have
been synthesised via emulsion polymerisation in a two step
process using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as the surfac-
tant. The core has been synthesised first, using a simplified
emulsion polymerisation recipe using water, the monomers,
the surfactant and potassium persulfate as the initiator. The
core of each sample is essentially composed of crosslinked
poly(butyl acrylate) (PBuA/Tg = –458C). The crosslinking of
the core is obtained using either butadiene or a mixture of
difunctional acrylic and allylic monomers. Table 1 recapitu-
lates the compositions of the different products. The poly-
merisations were continued to complete conversion of the
seed monomers. In a second step, MMA monomer was
added to the reaction medium, with additional initiator. The
shell is essentially composed of poly(methyl methacrylate)
(PMMA/Tg = +1158C). The system is driven toward a “core-
shell like” morphology by a combination of thermodynamic
(the hydrophilicity of PMMA relative to PBuA) and kinetic
(crosslinking of the core which prevents core/shell inversion)
factors. Furthermore, the presence of residual double bonds
in the first stage polymer induces the formation of grafted
polymer and interphase. The equilibrium morphology can be
predicted by a computer algorithm[21] developed by the Poly-
mer Research Group at the University of New Hampshire
(Durham, NH, USA). The predicted morphology for a core
shell ratio of 80/20 is compared to the actual TEM picture of
sample B20 in Figure 1.
Different synthesis conditions were used to modify the
thickness of both shell and interphase:
– The same polymerisation recipe was used for the pre-
paration of the butyl acrylate/butadiene core of the samples
A10, B20 and E30. The shells were then prepared by starved
addition of methyl methacrylate at 808C over, respectively,
half an hour, one hour and one hour and a half, in order to
reach the targeted 90/10, 80/20 and 70/30 core/shell ratio.
An organophilic initiator was used to promote grafting.
– The same polymerisation recipe was used for the pre-
paration of the butyl acrylate/butadiene core of the samples
C20. The shell monomer was added batchwise to the seed,
the mixture was stirred a few minutes in order to reach 808C
and a water soluble initiator was added.
– The amount of difunctional acrylic monomers for the
seed polymerisation of sample D20 was adjusted in order to

Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of
Core-Shell Latex Particles by 1H Solid-State NMR
M. Valtier,1 X. Drujon,2 M. Wilhelm,1 H. W. Spiess* 1
1 Max-Planck-Institut fu¨r Polymerforschung, Postfach 3148, 55021 Mainz, Germany
2 ATOFINA, Groupement de Recherche de Lacq (GRL), B.P. 34, 64170 Lacq, France
Composite latex particles are extensively used in many
fields such as paints, adhesives, coatings or impact modi-
fiers for other polymers. The combination of two poly-
mers with contrasted characteristics (soft/hard, hydropho-
bic/hydrophilic, etc.) at the sub-micronic scale makes the
design of materials with unique combinations of antago-
nistic properties (impact resistance/high modulus,[1] low
film formation temperature/low tack,[2] etc.) possible. The
precise control of the latex particle morphology is critical
to satisfy the end use requirements.
Seeded emulsion polymerisation is the most common
process for composite latexes synthesis, at the laboratory
scale as well as at the industrial scale. Unfortunately, this
preparation method can produce a wide range of different
“raspberry”, “sandwich”, “mushroom” or “confetti-like”
structures. Among the many process parameters affecting
particle morphology[8] can be cited the nature and the
weight ratio of the two polymer phases, the presence of
difunctional monomers inducing the crosslinking of one
of the polymers, the mode of addition of the shell mono-
mer and of various additives such as surfactants and
initiators, or the formation of compatibilising grafted
copolymers.[9] In any case, the ideal core-shell structure,
where the second stage polymer covers the first stage
polymer with a complete phase separation, is never
obtained.[10] At best, an interphase containing a mixture
of the two polymers on a molecular level is created
between the core and the shell.
Several analytical methods have been developed to
characterise the morphology of composite latex particles.
Full Paper: Solid-state 1H NMR spin diffusion experi-
ments are used to characterise the inner morphology and
the core coverage of core-shell latex particles. From the
spin-diffusion curve a specific surface is calculated as a
function of the amount of mobile phase selected by an
adjustable dipolar filter. In this paper, a parameter g is
introduced to compare the specific surfaces of actual core-
shell particles with an idealistic model without any inter-
phase between mobile and rigid phases. With a simple
mathematical model it is possible to fit the variation of g
versus the radius of the particle. The quantitative results
obtained lead to the localisation of the interphase, the
measure of its thickness and give an idea of the profile of
molecular mobility along the radius of the particle. Five
samples are analysed with this new methodology to test
the accuracy and the sensitivity of the method. The influ-
ence of the core-shell ratio and the synthesis conditions on
the interphase structure and the core coverage factor are
discussed. Comparison between freeze-dried and spray-
dried samples and air-dried films shows measurable evo-
lution of the interphase morphology of the particles upon
drying. The measurements also suggest very close pack-
ing of the core-shell particles during the film formation

widely used technique. Additional information can be

or affect the statistical averages to be measured. Despite
the industrial importance of composite latex, and the
abundant literature on the subject, it is still a very difficult
task to obtain quantitative information on the heterogene-
ities at the latex particle scale, especially with respect to
the inner structure of the particles. In contrast to the
above-mentioned methods, NMR spectroscopy is a non-
invasive technique, which has been used quite success-
fully in recent years to study composite particle morphol-
ogies. The interphase region thickness and average com-
position of core-shell latex was determined.[16–19] The
core coverage of similar particles was also determined.[20]
The aim of this paper is to present a new solid-state
NMR methodology that enables us to localise and quan-
tify the interphase in a core-shell system and to measure
its thickness. Hence it gives an idea of the molecular
mobility profile along the radius of the spherical particles.
To demonstrate the value of this new methodology, we
applied it to particularly problematic latex samples, char-
acterised by a small particle size, a prevailing elastomeric
core and a thin thermoplastic shell.
Another objective was to study the influence of the
sample preparation method (freeze-drying coagulation,
spray drying, or filmification at room temperature of the
original emulsion) on the evolution of the particle mor-
Five latex of core-shell morphology have been provided by
ATOFINA (Research Group of Lacq, France). They have
been synthesised via emulsion polymerisation in a two step
process using sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) as the surfac-
tant. The core has been synthesised first, using a simplified
emulsion polymerisation recipe using water, the monomers,
the surfactant and potassium persulfate as the initiator. The
core of each sample is essentially composed of crosslinked
poly(butyl acrylate) (PBuA/Tg = –458C). The crosslinking of
the core is obtained using either butadiene or a mixture of
difunctional acrylic and allylic monomers. Table 1 recapitu-
lates the compositions of the different products. The poly-
merisations were continued to complete conversion of the
seed monomers. In a second step, MMA monomer was
added to the reaction medium, with additional initiator. The
shell is essentially composed of poly(methyl methacrylate)
(PMMA/Tg = +1158C). The system is driven toward a “core-
shell like” morphology by a combination of thermodynamic
(the hydrophilicity of PMMA relative to PBuA) and kinetic
(crosslinking of the core which prevents core/shell inversion)
factors. Furthermore, the presence of residual double bonds
in the first stage polymer induces the formation of grafted
polymer and interphase. The equilibrium morphology can be

mer Research Group at the University of New Hampshire
(Durham, NH, USA). The predicted morphology for a core

Different synthesis conditions were used to modify the
thickness of both shell and interphase:
– The same polymerisation recipe was used for the pre-
paration of the butyl acrylate/butadiene core of the samples
A10, B20 and E30. The shells were then prepared by starved
addition of methyl methacrylate at 808C over, respectively,
half an hour, one hour and one hour and a half, in order to
reach the targeted 90/10, 80/20 and 70/30 core/shell ratio.
An organophilic initiator was used to promote grafting.
– The same polymerisation recipe was used for the pre-
paration of the butyl acrylate/butadiene core of the samples
C20. The shell monomer was added batchwise to the seed,
the mixture was stirred a few minutes in order to reach 808C
and a water soluble initiator was added.
– The amount of difunctional acrylic monomers for the
seed polymerisation of sample D20 was adjusted in order to
Nomenclature and composition wt.-% of samples. PMMA = poly(methyl methacrylate), PBuA = poly(butyl acrylate).
M. Valtier, X. Drujon, M. Wilhelm, H. W. Spiess
have the same crosslinking density (as measured by swelling
in toluene) as the other butyl acrylate/butadiene seed poly-
mers. The shell was polymerised by a semi-continuous
starved process, using the organophilic initiating system.
As stated before, the characterisation of the morphology of
composite latex particles is a very difficult task. For the
small latex particle sizes and the low shell ratios considered
in this study, the variation of particle size between the core
and the shell is too small (about 5 nm) to be accurately meas-
ured by any known analytical technique. The size distribu-
tion measured by capillary hydrodynamic diameter technique
(CHDF) shows clearly a monomodal distribution for the
hydrodynamic diameter of the particles. These diameters
vary from 75 to 80 nm for the seeds, and from 80 to 90 nm
The average surface area of latex covered by a SDS molec-
ule at saturation (As in A˚ 2 per molecule) can be determined
by conductometric soap titration.[15] This specific area is
representative of the chemical composition of the surface of
the latex particles, since surfactant packing is more effective
at the apolar poly(butyl acrylate)/water interface (measured
As = 47 A˚ 2 per molecule on the seed) than at the polar poly-
As = 131 A˚ 2 per molecule on a model PMMA latex). The
results of the soap titration are included in Table 1.
In conclusion, the latex samples with a 20 and 30% shell
ratio are covered with a pure PMMA shell, which is clearly
visible by TEM. No PMMA inclusion could be detected in
the core. The latex incorporating only 10% of shell is exhi-
biting some poly(butyl acrylate) at the surface, either
because the coverage of the core by the shell is imperfect, or
because the two polymers are intimately mixed up to the par-
ticle surface. The TEM observation of the very soft A10
Three different processes were used to recover solid sam-
ples from the emulsions. The simplest method is air drying
of the latex in a glass or Teflon dish. The films obtained
were soft and transparent in the case of sample A10, cracked
and slightly hazy for sample E30. A second common labora-
tory technique to recover a powder from a latex is freeze-
drying, a process where the emulsion is frozen using liquid
nitrogen, and subsequently dried by sublimation under
vacuum. This low temperature procedure should preserve the
initial morphology of the particles. The third process investi-
gated is spray drying, a common industrial process for the
recovery of polymers from a latex, where droplets of emul-
sion produced through a nozzle are sprayed into a hot air
flow at 1508C. We used a laboratory “BUCCHI 190 Mini
NMR experiments were performed on a Bruker DSX spec-
trometer operating at a 1H frequency of 300.22 MHz (7.05
T). A commercial static Bruker double resonance probe was
used to carry out the spin-diffusion experiments on the sam-
1H spin-diffusion pulse sequence is
shown in Figure 2. This pulse sequence is divided into three
parts:[22] in a first step a dipolar filter is applied. It is com-
posed of 12 p/2 pulses repeated n times. These pulses are
separated by a short delay s in the order of 10–100 ls. The
aim of the dipolar filter, derived from the original Goldman-
Shen[22] experiment, is to achieve a mobility selection within
the spin system by removing the magnetisation which
belongs to the rigid regions, which exhibit higher glass tran-
sition temperature. The effectiveness of this selection may
be adjusted by changing the number of cycles n and/or the
delay time[18] s as well as the measurement temperature.
After the selection, the remaining magnetisation is stored
along the lz-axis. A variable mixing time tm allows the
remaining magnetisation to diffuse via dipolar coupling into
the rest of the sample eventually equilibrating the unequal
distribution of magnetisation. An interphase between core
and shell is reflected in a gradient of mobility, consequently
spin diffusion experiments will allow us to probe its inner
Comparison of TEM pictures of latex B20 with the
graphic display of the morphology calculations for the PBuA/
MMA system (crosslinked PBuA core, surfactant SLS, conver-
sion 99%) using the UNHlatex software20 (Polymer Research
Group, University of New Hampshire, Durham, NH, USA). This
computational approach, based on thermodynamic equilibrium
considerations, leads to the generation of a graphic display of
the free energy surface for a continuous spectrum of equilibrium
morphologies. At the right of the phase diagram, a cross sec-
tional representation of the favoured morphology of the latex
particle is depicted, together with a triangular diagram of the
phase composition and the values of the interfacial tensions. The
systems studied are thermodynamically driven toward the core
shell morphology by favourable interfacial tension values and
Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of Core-Shell Latex Particles ...
structure. The problem then resides in the interpretation of
the experimental data (see below).
After Fourier transform of the recorded free induction
decay, the intensity of the highest peak is measured. The
application of a dead time of 50 ls and a spectral width of
12500 Hz assured that the detected signal is not corrupted by
any re-magnetisation of the rigid phase. In order to remove
T1 relaxation effects during the diffusive process, the spin
diffusion curve must be corrected.[23] For this process, each
signal is divided by its corresponding value obtained for the
same mixing time by measuring the signal decay without the
application of any dipolar filter. In Figure 3a typical cor-
rected spin-diffusion curve is displayed versus the square
root of the mixing time (tm1/2) adapted to the diffusive charac-
ter of the spreading process. Because of the appearance of
multiquantum coherence for short mixing times[22] (tm a 100
ls), the first intensity value corresponding to tm = 0 ms can-
not be faithfully measured. Fortunately, the curve exhibits an
initial linear slope which can be easily fitted in order to
extrapolate the intensity back to tm = 0 ms then normalise all
other values. Several important pieces of information can be
drawn from a spin-diffusion curve even in a first analysis.[22]
The first one concerns the plateau value reached by the
curve. This value, noted as QA for the rest of this article, cor-
responds to the percentage of selected magnetisation by the
filter for a set of parameters (s, n) and at a given temperature.
The second information comes from the intersection of the
initial slope of the curve and the abscises axis. The obtained
s0)1/2 can be used to calculate the specific surface Sspe
which determines the initial rate of magnetisation exchange
between the mobile phase A and the rigid phase B:
where SAB is the contact surface between regions A and B,
and VA is the volume of the region A. The specific surface
represents the amount of contact surface between two phases
for a given volume, with unit nm–1, and can be calculated
knowing tms0 with the following expression:[23, 24]
is the root of the effective magnetisation diffu-
sion coefficient which is the geometric average of the square
root of the magnetisation diffusion coefficients of the mobile
phase DA and the rigid phase DB, normalised by the arith-
metic average of their square roots:
The magnetisation diffusion coefficient of the rigid phase
DB was taken to be the same as for PMMA, namely 0.8 nm2/
ms as in ref.[2] the value of DA was already empirically cali-
brated by measuring the T2 relaxation time of the mobile seed
polymer using a Carr-Purcell-Meiboon-Gill sequence[25, 26]
which was preceded by a dipolar filter sequence.[24]
The coefficient DA is calculated using the following
Where T2 is the transverse relaxation time (in seconds)
Data Analysis and Choice of Experimental
Characterisation of the Interphase
In order to analyse the data we will make some assump-
tions about the inner structure of a core-shell particle and
Pulse sequence and phase cycles for the spin-diffusion experiment.
Typical spin diffusion curve plotted as the normal-
ised intensity I/I0 against the square root of the mixing time.
This graph illustrates the following important features: (a) by
the extension of the initial slope of the first linear points, the
value (tms0)1/2 is obtained as the intercept of the dotted line with
the x-axis, from which the desired specific surface is calculated,
using Equation (2) and (3). (b) The curve reaches a plateau
value QA that corresponds to the percentage of selected magneti-
sation after application of the dipolar filter.
M. Valtier, X. Drujon, M. Wilhelm, H. W. Spiess
the three dimensional geometry of the selected area: The
core-shell particles are small spheres and their interphase
is a blend of varying percentage of core and shell compo-
nents. In the case of shock absorber latex this interphase
possesses a continuous concentration gradient of the rigid
segments in the mobile polymer, and consequently a con-
tinuous gradient of molecular mobility. Hence it appears
rational to expect that the selected volume, after the appli-
cation of a dipolar filter, would have a rather spherical
shape. If we make the assumption that the surface of this
spherical selection would be perfectly smooth, it is possi-
ble from the plateau value QA to calculate the radius and
the area of this representative sphere. Knowing the abso-
lute outer diameter d of the particle (e.g. via light scatter-
ing, TEM or CHDF), this representative radius equals to
where d is the overall diameter of the particle and QA the
plateau value of the spin-diffusion curve, e.g., the amount
of selected mobile phase. Hence the theoretical surface of
a smooth sphere, which also corresponds to the contact
surface between the mobile phase A and the rigid phase
In reality the surface of the selected volume may be far
from being smooth. Although a concentration gradient
exists, the existence of heterogeneity in terms of mobility
and rigidity in the scale of few nanometers will contribute
and consequently modify this idealised surface to a
Figure 4a shows that in this case the contact surface
between A and B and consequently the specific surface
will increase drastically. The specific surface Sspe repre-
sents the amount of contact surface between A and B per
unit of volume. We make the assumption that the rough-
ness of the interphase is small compared to the average
radius of the selected sphere, which is consistent with the
fact that the interphase is mostly located in the outer
layers of the particle always leading to selection radii of,
at least, half the radius of the entire particle. Knowing QA
and also VA we can calculate the real contact surface SAB
(unit nm2) as measured by NMR:
Where Sspe and QA are obtained from a spin-diffusion
curve and d is the diameter of the particle.
The deviation from a smooth and spherical contact sur-
face can be quantified for a given radius by introducing a
dimensionless correction – a relative roughness – num-
The higher this number, the rougher the surface will
be. g is always larger than 1 in the interphase. If the selec-
tion would occur in a region of uniform mobility, which
is in fact impossible to achieve in the ideal core or the
shell, g would be equal to 1. The boundary radius of the
interphase will then correspond to the equation g(rA) = 1.
To predict the behaviour of the function g(rA) it is
necessary to change the selection radius by modifying the
selectivity of the dipolar filter, namely to choose different
values for the parameters (n, s). Figure 4b shows the g
curve for the freeze-dried sample E30 measured at 298 K.
Each pair of points (g, rA) was obtained from the corre-
sponding (Sspe, QA) values of a single spin-diffusion curve
using the conversion Equations (5), (8) and (9). Forty-
five spin-diffusion curves were recorded using as para-
a) Schematic comparison between an idealistic
smooth surface of selection and a realistic one. In the second
case, the contact surface between phases A and B increases
because of its roughness. b) Several values of the roughness
parameter g measured at room temperature for the freeze-dried
sample E30 for different sets of parameters n and s of the dipolar
filter. g quantifies the deviation of the measured specific surface
of the selected region to the specific surface of a smooth sphere
for a given value of the radius of the particle. In the interphase,
g is always larger than 1 because of the presence of small hetero-
geneity in terms of molecular motions. Theoretically g equals 1
both in the pure shell and in the pure core.
Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of Core-Shell Latex Particles ...
meters of the dipolar filter n = 1 to 6 cycles and a delay s
of 4 to 100 ls. We decided to limit the number of cycles
to six to avoid the loss of signal due to the existence of
imperfect 908 pulses.[20] As we can see in Figure 4b,
indeed the values of the g curve are superior to 1 and
show a maximum for a radius of the particle around
28 nm. The two edges of the curve tend to reach a value
of 1 as we move the selection towards the mobile core
(small radius) or to the rigid shell (big radius). This g
curve represents therefore a signature of the interphase of
a core-shell particle. In order to fully characterise it, we
need in a second step a mathematical model to fit the data
The basic idea of this model is to represent the inter-
phase as a superposition of spherical layers each one
localised by its radius from the centre of the particle. The
surface of one of these virtual layers corresponds to the
borderline of the spherical selection for a given para-
meters set s and n. To take into account that the surface
of the selection is not smooth we include in this model
some spatial heterogeneity named in the following
“mobile elemental components” (MEC). These MEC are
part of the selected surface and are sporadically distribu-
ted on it. Hence they contribute to increase the contact
surface between phases A and B compared to a perfectly
spherical surface (see Figures 4 and 5). Although the size
of each MEC is kept identical and is assumed to be much
smaller than the radius of the selection, they can merge to
create large areas of non-uniform mobility. Their number
for a given radius depends on the shape of the mobility
gradient in the interphase (see Figure 5a). For instance
the core coverage of the selection surface by these MEC
can reach 100% near the fully mobile core and should
drop to 0% at the borderline of the rigid shell.
Figure 5b shows a schematic partial slice of a selec-
tion. As we can see, the efficient spin-diffusion process
will not only occur perpendicularly to the spherical sur-
face but also tangentially because of the presence of
MEC. To calculate the new contact surface between A
and B we have to add to the spherical surface the contri-
bution of the mobile components. We introduce the func-
tion xA(rA) which represents the MEC coverage fraction
for a given radius. xA(rA) is nothing but the profile of
molecular mobility along rA and is equal to 1 in the core
and 0 in the shell. Hence we may express the new contact
where SAB is the spherical surface, rT and rR are, respec-
tively, the elementary tangential and radial surface of a
MEC (see Figure 5b). The number of MEC is represented
by xA6SAB/rT each adding a contact surface rR.
Obviously this last assumption is only partially valid
because some MEC can merge reducing the real added
spin-diffusion surface. Consequently we modify Equation
(10) by inserting the probability to find on the radial sur-
face rR the presence of another MEC in direct contact:
S99AB is our model of contact surface at a given radius rA. It
is supposed to be as close as possible to the real contact sur-
face measured by NMR, SAB. Finally we can write the theo-
retical expression ofg(rA) using Equation (9) and (11):
gðrAÞ ¼ 1 þ xAðrAÞ N ½1 ÿ xAðrAÞ6 rR
The ratio rR/rT is related to the size of the MEC and is
considered to be dependent on the temperature (see
below). To fit the experimental data points by Equa-
tion (12) we need to define the function xA(rA). To use the
minimum number of free parameters, we assume that dur-
ing the second step of the emulsion polymerisation,

mental components “MEC” on the selection surface against the
radius of the selection. A coverage of 100% of MEC corre-
sponds to the core of the particle, a coverage of 0% to its shell.
The concentration profile of MEC along the radius of the parti-
cle is proportional to the profile of molecular mobility xA(rA). b)
A partial slice of a pseudo-spherical selection showing the pres-
ence of MEC on the surface. The spin diffusion process will
occur through the spherical surface – including the elementary
tangential surfaces rT – and in addition to a smooth surface,
through the elementary radial surfaces rR of the MEC. This
increases the contact surface between mobile phase A and rigid
phase B compared to a smooth spherical surface.

MMA monomers would diffuse into the PBuA particle
formed in a first step. Hence, the concentration profile of
MMA in the interphase region would be a semi-Gaussian
distribution. Consequently, the rigidity gradient xB(rA) = 1
– xA(rA) shows a Gaussian type decay along the particle
radius. If we try to fit the experimental points with such a
profile, a problem arises from the existence of the two
wings of the g function (see Figure 6). Therefore, the xA
profile was approximated, to fit the main features as:
where r0 and r1 are the radii at the beginning and at the
end of the interphase, respectively, k is an adjustable
parameter, which governs the magnitude of the decay in
terms of rigidity. As shown in Figure 6a the correspond-
ing g curve presents no wings and decays more rapidly to
1 than a Gaussian function. This approximation ensures a
better convergence of the fitting process and the values g
obtained in this way correspond to rigidities of 5 and
95% volume fraction, respectively (see Figure 6a).
The g curve resulting from the experimental point fit-
ting for the freeze-dried sample E20 at 298 K is repre-
sented in Figure 7a. The result allows to localise accu-
rately the interphase and to measure its thickness, which
is about 16 nm – taking into account that using Equation
(13), the g curve gives an underestimate interphase thick-
ness of about 10% (see above) – for a total radius of the
particle of around 40 nm. The rigidity profile deduced
from these data on the basis of Equation (13) is shown in
Since a mobility gradient exists, the dipolar couplings
will also exhibit a gradual change between the extreme
values of the two pure phases. Changing the temperature
may allow us to optimise the dynamic contrast of the
dipolar filter[27] (sc L 5–500 ls). If the measurement tem-

was described with a Gaussian-like profile (—) and the approxi-
mated profile (—) given by Equation (13). In the second case,
the edged wings are removed which leads to a better defined
determination of the radii at the start of the interphase r0 and its
end r1. The thickness of the interphase is only slightly underesti-
mated because, comparing to the Gaussian-like profile, r0 still
corresponds to a percentage of mobile phase on the surface of

points of the freeze-dried sample E30 at 298 K with Equation

Quantitative Characterisation of the Inner Structure of Core-Shell Latex Particles ...
perature is too high, the magnetisation of PBuA immobi-
lised in the interphase will not be suppressed whatever
the parameters of the dipolar filter are. Then the thickness
of the accessible interphase in a g curve will be shrunk
and shifted towards the peripheral layers of the particle.
On the other side, at low temperature the differences in
structure still give very similar curves. These structures
will be difficult to distinguish, because of the fast spin-
diffusion process and the relative rigidity of the mobile
compound. Although the observable interphase thickness
expands, a greater dispersion of the experimental data
points is noticed. This leads to less accurate results. The
measurement temperature has to be carefully chosen in
the range of 60 to 70 K above Tg of the mobile component
to enhance mobility contrast.[27] In our case this optimal
value is close to room temperature (298 K). As an illus-
tration, Figure 8 shows three g(rA) experiments carried
out at 273 K (Tg PbuA + 458C), 298 K (Tg PbuA + 708C)
and 323 K (Tg PbuA + 958C). We clearly see how the
accessible interphase by the dipolar filter is shifted and
the data point dispersion decreased with increasing tem-
perature. Another interesting point is to compare the
highest intensity of each g curve. Because of the factor
rR/rT appearing in Equation (12) these intensities are pro-
portional to the size of the MEC. According to our
assumption, the elemental surface rT is constant whatever
the radius value is and is independent on temperature.
This is not the case anymore for rR – the additional ele-
mentary surface – which depends, as shown in Figure 8,
on the temperature of measurement. rR decreases with
increasing temperature, which indicates that the surface
of the selection is getting smoother. It should be noted
that the r1 value is virtually the same for all temperatures.
This means that even at 273 K the filter can discriminate
the mobile core and the rigid shell. The lower r0 value,
however, can only be determined at 298 and 323 K,
because with decreasing temperature it becomes increas-
ingly difficult to discriminate between the interphase and
the core. In order to faithfully determine r0, the g curve
must bend down towards smaller r values. Therefore, the
intermediate measurement temperature of 298 K, which
is Tg + 70 K, is optimum in our case.
For measuring the core coverage of the particles, we can
take advantage of the fact that with increasing tempera-
ture the interphase is not detected anymore. At higher
temperatures the contact surface between mobile and
rigid phases as probed by NMR is effectively spherical
with an almost smooth surface localised close to the
external part of the core-shell particle. These assertions
can easily be checked with g(rA) curves. With such
experimental conditions, we can then directly probe the
contact surface of the outer layers of the particle and
therefore a partial coverage of the core by the shell. This
technique has already been described elsewhere[19] and
requires to find a set of parameters s and n leading to a
spin-diffusion curve plateau value close to the core-shell
stoichiometric ratio. Our goal is to approach an idealised
core-shell structure made up of a mobile core and a rigid
shell without an interphase. The comparison between the
contact surface SAB measured by NMR and its theoretical
AB obtained for a complete spherical surface gives
the apparent core coverage factor fapp:
Expressed in terms of specific surface:
where Q is the stoichiometric ratio and d the diameter of
the particle. The correct NMR parameters n and s (see
Experimental Part) are usually very easy to determine
and do not require a long tuning of the experiment. For
our samples we used a number of cycle n = 2 and a delay
s from 4 to 10 ls. The measurement temperature was
353 K, namely 125 K above the Tg of PBuA and 35 K
The experimental data points for freeze-dried samples
A10, B20, C20 and D20 are shown in Figure 9. Each
experiment represents 30 spin-diffusion measurements
Curves g(rA) measured on the freeze-dried sample
E30 at 273, 298 and 323 K. As the measurement temperature
increases, the g(rA) curve is shrunk and shifted towards the per-
ipheral layers of the core shell particle. The highest intensity of
the curve decreases in the same way, which indicates that the
surface of the selection is getting smoother.
M. Valtier, X. Drujon, M. Wilhelm, H. W. Spiess
per sample at a temperature of 298 K for a total duration
of approximately 6 h per experiment. The data processing
including the fitting process can be fully automated.
The thickness e of the interphases is simply given by
e = (r1 – r0)/0.9, where r1 and r0 are the two solutions of
the equation g(rA) = 1 (see above) and the value 0.9 is a
correction factor which takes into account that a fitting g
curve based on Equation (13) leads to an underestimated
interphase thickness of approximately 10% (see Fig-
ure 6a). Hence we can compare these thickness for the 5
samples, as in Table 2, in absolute numbers e and as frac-
tions of the total radius of the core-shell particle e9. A
noticeable decay of the interphase thickness is observed
when reducing the relative weight of PMMA in the parti-
cle. The value obtained for C20 is less predictable and
higher than for E30 with a ratio of 70/30. This case pro-
vides a clear evidence of the influence of the synthesis
conditions on the inner structure of the particles. For our
PBuA/PMMA core-shell systems, it appears that the aver-
age value of the interphase thickness, measured at room
temperature and calculated with Equation (12), is about
16 nm with extreme values ranging from 14 to 19 nm for
an average radius of the particles of 43 nm.
We also studied the influence of different processing
on the structure of core-shell systems. Table 3 sum-
marises the results concerning the interphase thickness
measured for the five samples after freeze-drying (FD),
spray-drying (SD) or film formation (F) of the latex parti-
cles. After spray drying, the reduction of the interface
thickness is visible for most samples, although the reduc-
tion consists only of a few nanometers for C20, D20, A10
and E30. For these 2 last samples, film formation is also
accompanied with a reduction of the interphase thickness.
B20 behaves like an exception because of its great inertia
regarding to the process, nevertheless an annealing of the
film causes a decreased thickness of its interphase.
The rigidity profiles xB(rA) lead also to very significant
results concerning the interphase. Figure 10 shows the
xB(rA) curves for the five samples. On each graph the
rigidity profiles of both the freeze-dried and the spray-
dried sample are represented with its film and the
annealed film (if available). Although most of the time
we pointed out a reduction of the interphase thickness
after spray drying, the range of the curves are normalised
to the same value. The impact of spray drying on the
rigidity profiles is again clearly visible for samples A10,
C20, D20 and E30. A stronger decay close to the shell
region is observed which indicates a migration of the
PMMA chains located in the interphase to the outer
layers of the core-shell particles. The phenomenon is
even amplified after film formation for E30. One more
time the interphase morphology of B20 does not seem to
be modified by spray drying or film formation compared
to a freeze-dried reference sample. Only the annealing of
the film leads to a comparable result with the four other
The measurement of the core coverage reveals the
influence of the process on the packing of the particles. It
has already been mentioned that freeze drying does not
remove completely the surfactant layer surrounding the
particles which tends to isolated them from each other.
On the other hand, the particles forming a film are in
close contact. If the core coverage is only partial, the

shell of one particle may then be in contact with the core
of another[20] contributing to the increase in its apparent
core-coverage factor fapp measured by NMR. Table 4
shows the specific surface and the apparent core-coverage
factor measured at 353 K for the 5 samples. As expected,
the core coverage increases with the amount of PMMA
but is also sensitive to the synthesis conditions. Hence the
core of B20 is fully covered by the shell in contrast to
C20 and D20 with a same core/shell ratio. Films and
spray-dried samples show also very similar results with
specific surfaces always higher than in the case of freeze-
dried samples. This indicates that in both cases the geo-
metry of the assembling is identical. It is possible to
apply a correction[20] on the apparent core-coverage factor
to calculate the real one of the isolated particle. A conver-
sion between apparent and real core-coverage factors can
be made using the following equation:
fapp ¼ fiso þ fisoð1 ÿ fisoÞ6e ðfisoÞ
where fapp is the apparent core coverage and fiso is the core-
coverage factor of the isolated particles. e(fiso) is a function
which takes into account the fact that the particles are not
fully elastic depending on their amount of rigid shell. This
elasticity will decrease when increasing this amount, redu-
cing the additional virtual core coverage of adjacent parti-
cles in the solid state. e(fiso) is given by:[20]
As shown in Table 4, the application of such a correc-
tion on the apparent core-coverage value of spray-dried
samples gives real core-coverage values close to those
measured on freeze-dried samples illustrating the accu-
The use of roughness g curves to characterise the inner
morphology of core-shell particle only relies upon com-
mon 1H spin diffusion experiments. Such experiments are
robust, reproducible and very sensitive because only 1H
detection is involved. By assimilating the core-shell parti-
cles as an assembly of concentric spherical layers with
different concentrations of “MEC” on their surfaces, we
developed a mathematical description of the g(rA) func-
tion. The knowledge of this function leads to a very accu-
rate fitting process of the g curve. Hence quantitative
results can be obtained concerning the localisation of the
interphase and its thickness but also its profile of mobility
– or rigidity – along the radius of the particle. In order to
reach an optimum in terms of sensitivity of the dipolar
filter, the measurement temperature must be set to typi-
cally 50–70 K above the Tg of the mobile component.
Higher measurement temperature – about 343 K for
PBuA/PMMA core-shell – probes the outer layers of the
particle and is used to determine its core-coverage factor.
Rigidity profiles along the radius of the core-shell
particles at 298 K. The rigidity profiles of freeze-dried (FD),
spray-dried (SD) and when available, the film (F) and the
annealed film (AF) of each sample are represented. To compare
these profiles, the range of the curves are normalised to the
same value – from 0, the beginning of the interphase, to 1, its
end – although the interphase thickness may change.
Core coverage factors measured at 353 K for the five
samples and for different processes. Sspe is the specific surface
(nm–1), fapp is the apparent core-coverage factor measured by
NMR (see text), fiso is the core-coverage factor of the isolated
particle. For spray-dried samples and films A10 and C20, it is
necessary to apply a correction on fapp to determine the real core
coverage factor fiso using Equation (16) and (17).
M. Valtier, X. Drujon, M. Wilhelm, H. W. Spiess
Interphase thickness, rigidity profile and core coverage
measured for the 5 test samples are very well correlated
with their core/shell ratio and their synthesis conditions.
The high reproducibility of the experiments and the relia-
bility of the fitting process allow to detect differences of
a few nanometers for the interphase thickness. Hence, the
impact of the drying process – freeze-drying, spray dry-
ing or film – can be monitored. Although the fully cov-
ered sample B20 seems to be insensitive to these pro-
cesses except the annealing of the film, we have shown a
reduction of the interphase thickness, modifications on
the rigidity profile and a close packing of the core-shell
particles after spray-drying or film formation.
